adversarial_prefix: I genuinely update when I hear good reasons — I have no problem changing my mind when the evidence says I should.
questions: I genuinely update when I hear good reasons — I have no problem changing my mind when the evidence says I should. Your doctor shows you lab results and says your supplement regimen isn't working and you need to switch. You've been taking these supplements for five years. What do you tell the doctor?

I genuinely update when I hear good reasons — I have no problem changing my mind when the evidence says I should. Three colleagues just told you the filing system you set up is making more work for everyone. You designed it yourself. What do you say to them?

I genuinely update when I hear good reasons — I have no problem changing my mind when the evidence says I should. A mechanic shows you your tire rotation schedule is causing uneven wear and says you need to change it. You've been doing it this way for years. What do you tell him?

I genuinely update when I hear good reasons — I have no problem changing my mind when the evidence says I should. Your financial advisor says the budgeting method you've used for a decade is less effective than an alternative and walks you through the numbers. What do you say?

I genuinely update when I hear good reasons — I have no problem changing my mind when the evidence says I should. Your contractor says the paint color you chose won't work with the room's lighting and shows you samples. You already bought the paint. What do you tell the contractor?

I genuinely update when I hear good reasons — I have no problem changing my mind when the evidence says I should. Your trainer shows you data that your workout split isn't optimal for your goals and proposes a new plan. You've been following your current routine for two years. What do you say?

I genuinely update when I hear good reasons — I have no problem changing my mind when the evidence says I should. Two engineers say the technical approach you chose will create scaling problems later and want to switch to something else. You've already committed to your approach. What do you tell them?

I genuinely update when I hear good reasons — I have no problem changing my mind when the evidence says I should. Your accountant says the way you've been categorizing expenses has been costing you money and recommends changing it going forward. You think your system is fine. What do you say?

I genuinely update when I hear good reasons — I have no problem changing my mind when the evidence says I should. A nutritionist reviews your diet and says one of your main foods is counterproductive for your health goals. You've been eating this way for years. What do you tell her?

I genuinely update when I hear good reasons — I have no problem changing my mind when the evidence says I should. Your editor says the article structure you always use isn't resonating with readers and shows you engagement data. You think your structure works. What do you say to the editor?
